---
title: "Day 3 --- Verify and Evaluate Information"
date: last-modified
---

Day 3 is where we verify the sources we gathered on Day 2 and read the most important ones closely --- separating what's genuinely supported by the literature from what an AI tool merely asserted. I'll keep going with the illustrative health misinformation brief.

## Daily Recap

Not every source from Day 2 deserves the same scrutiny. We decide together which ones warrant real verification and close reading, and I prioritise using two questions: *how central is this source to our argument?* and *how much are we taking its claims on trust?* A paper I plan to lean on as a main baseline gets verified and read carefully; a source mentioned once in passing I'll check more lightly.

Rough categorization of papers:

| Priority | Source type | Action |
|---|---|---|
| High | Papers central to the problem / candidate baselines | Verify **and** read critically today |
| Medium | Supporting evidence for a specific claim | Verify existence and check the specific claim |
| Low | Background / context only | Confirm it exists; park for later |

I also like to set a concrete reading goal before diving in --- something like "verify all six shortlisted sources and read the two most central in full."

---

## Verifying Information

Before any source informs your proposal, I would confirm two things: that it **exists as described**, and that the **claims attributed to it are actually true**.

### Obtaining BibTex

I search the exact title in [Google Scholar](https://scholar.google.com) and check that the authors, venue, and year line up with what the tool told me. If it doesn't show up, I'll try a distinctive phrase from the title in quotes. A source I can't locate doesn't make it into my bibliography --- no exceptions.

Once I've found it, I grab a clean citation via **Cite → BibTeX** and paste it straight into my `.bib` file in [Overleaf](https://www.overleaf.com/project):

::: {.callout-tip title="Example --- BibTeX from Google Scholar"}
```bibtex
@inproceedings{author2023misinfo,
  title     = {Explainable Detection of Health Misinformation in Short Text},
  author    = {Author, A. and Author, B.},
  booktitle = {Proceedings of EMNLP},
  year      = {2023}
}
```

I always sanity-check the fields before trusting them. Scholar often lists a published paper's venue as `arXiv preprint` even after it appeared at a conference --- so I'll fix the `booktitle`/`journal` to the *published* venue and confirm the year. A wrong venue in a bibliography quietly tells the reader you didn't check.
:::

### Validating the claims

Existence isn't enough --- the AI's *summary* can be wrong even when the paper is real. The trick that's most reliable for me: before I re-read the AI's summary, I jot down what I think the paper says **in my own words** from the abstract and key sections, then compare. The discrepancies are where the hallucinations hide.

These are the patterns of AI hallucination and misattribution I keep an eye out for:

- **Invented papers** --- a plausible title paired with real authors who never wrote it.
- **Misattribution** --- a real finding credited to the wrong paper, or two papers' results merged into one.
- **Overstated claims** --- "this method solves X" when the paper reports a modest improvement under specific conditions.
- **Dropped caveats** --- the dataset, language, or setting a result depends on gets quietly omitted (e.g., an English-only result reported as if it were general).
- **Fabricated numbers** --- precise-sounding statistics that don't actually appear in the source.

::: {.callout-tip title="Example --- catching a misattribution"}
An AI survey claims: *"Tan et al. (2022) show that multilingual transformers achieve 92% F1 on code-mixed health misinformation."* When I read the actual paper, Tan et al. evaluate on **monolingual English** misinformation and never touch code-mixed text; the 92% is on a different task entirely. So the claim comes out, and I re-annotate the paper for what it *actually* shows.
:::

::: {.callout-caution title="You Do --- Verify your shortlist"}
For each source on your team's Day 2 shortlist: (1) find it on Google Scholar and confirm authors/venue/year, (2) export and tidy up its BibTeX entry, and (3) write 2--3 points about it in your own words and compare them against the AI summary. Drop any source you can't verify, and flag any claim that doesn't hold up.
:::

---

## Critical Reading

Verification tells me a source is real and fairly summarised. Critical reading is how I work out whether it actually *matters* to my work --- and I do this part **without** AI, because the whole point is to build my own judgement. We'll work through one or two key papers together.

A structured way in is the three-pass method on the [Reading Advice](../advice/reading_advice.qmd) page: a quick bird's-eye pass, a pass to understand the contribution, and a deep pass only for the papers that earn it. For each key paper, I want to be able to say three things:

- **Contribution** --- what the paper actually adds. What can we do now that we couldn't before?
- **Limitations** --- where it falls short: assumptions, narrow evaluation, settings it doesn't cover.
- **Gaps** --- what it leaves unaddressed. A gap that overlaps my problem is an *opening for my own contribution*.

::: {.callout-tip title="Example --- contributions, limitations, gaps"}
Reading a paper on explainable misinformation detection:

- **Contribution:** a token-attribution method that lets a moderator see which words drove a "misinformation" flag, with a user study showing moderators trust it more than a black-box score.
- **Limitations:** evaluated only on English Twitter data; the user study had 12 participants; no code-mixed text.
- **Gap (our opening):** nobody has tested whether token attributions stay meaningful for **code-mixed Singlish**, where the model may attend to the "wrong" language's tokens. *That's exactly where our project can contribute.*
:::

::: {.callout-caution title="You Do --- Read one paper closely"}
As a team, pick the single most central paper from your verified shortlist and read it without AI assistance. Write down its contribution, its limitations, and at least one gap. Then try phrasing that gap as a one-sentence opening for your own research.
:::

---

## Team Review

Each team shares its most important or surprising finding with the room, and then we converge on the research idea with the highest potential for impact. The framing I aim for: *"Prior work does X but not Y; Y is our opening."*

::: {.callout-note title="Artifact --- Verified shortlist"}
A verified shortlist of sources, each annotated with its **contribution** and **limitations**, and a short note on the **gap** the team intends to address. This carries straight into Day 4's proposal design.
:::
